home.subtitle: Watch your code run, step by step and learn algorithms intuitively.
home.playgroundDesc: Paste algorithm code or write your own.
home.customCardDesc: Supports JS / TS. Functions and parameters are detected automatically.
home.sortingSub: Explore how different sorting algorithms work step by step.
home.recursionSub: Visualize recursive call flows as a tree.

visualizer.variablesEmpty: Run a step to see Variables

custom.hint: 💡 Paste JS / TS code and we'll detect functions and parameters automatically. Enter values and run!

editor.placeholder: Paste your algorithm code or write your own

selection-sort.name: Selection Sort
selection-sort.description: Finds the smallest element and moves it to the front

insertion-sort.name: Insertion Sort
insertion-sort.description: Inserts each element into its correct position

quick-sort.name: Quick Sort
quick-sort.description: Partitions around a pivot and sorts recursively

merge-sort.name: Merge Sort
merge-sort.description: Divides in half and merges back in order

permutations.name: Permutations
permutations.description: Select r items from n in order

combinations.name: Combinations
combinations.description: Select r items from n without order

subsets.name: Subsets
subsets.description: Find all subsets of the given array

docs.title: Docs
docs.subtitle: What Recursive can do and how to use it.
docs.feat1Desc: Paste your code and input fields for function parameters appear automatically.
docs.feat2Desc: If there's a recursive function, the call flow is drawn as a tree. See the order of calls and returns at a glance.
docs.feat3Desc: The current line is highlighted. Step through manually or auto-play. No breakpoints needed.
docs.feat4Desc: Variable values are shown at every step. Arrays appear as cells, and changed values are highlighted.
docs.feat5Desc: A single function works, and so does plain code with no functions at all.
docs.pythonDesc: The standard library is fully available. collections (Counter, defaultdict), functools (@cache, @lru_cache), itertools — all work.
docs.jsDesc: TypeScript types are stripped automatically. Math, JSON, Array methods, and other built-in APIs work out of the box.
docs.step3: If a function is detected, parameter fields appear. Enter values.
docs.step5: Step through line by line or auto-play. Watch variables change in the panel.
docs.presetDesc: The Algorithm Study page has presets for sorting (bubble, selection, insertion, quick, merge) and recursion (permutations, combinations, subsets). Try them out.
docs.embedDesc1: Paste this into your blog or Obsidian and the visualization will show up.
docs.embedDesc2: In Notion, type /embed and paste the URL below.
docs.embedDesc3: On any preset page, hit the Embed button to copy the code.
docs.heroStar: If this helped, a <link>GitHub ⭐</link> would be appreciated!
docs.heroSponsor: Your <link>sponsorship</link> helps support more languages like Java and C++.
docs.tip1Desc: Just paste your solution code, function and all. Comments are fine, and parameters are detected automatically. Fill in the inputs and hit run.
docs.tip1Hint: Try pressing the buttons. In the actual app, you can modify the input values.
docs.tip2Title: Plain code without a function works too
docs.tip2Desc: Code without a wrapping function runs just fine. No parameter inputs needed — just hit ▶ Run.
docs.tip3Title: Embed in your blog or Notion
docs.tip3Desc: You can embed the execution view in your blog, Notion, Obsidian, or anywhere that supports iframes. Click the Embed button at the top of the execution view to copy the iframe code. You can adjust the height too, and for Notion just use /embed and paste the URL.
docs.tip4Python: Python supports the standard library freely (collections, functools, itertools, etc).
docs.tip4Js: JavaScript / TypeScript types are stripped automatically. Built-in APIs like Math, JSON, and Array methods all work.
docs.limit6: Java, C, C++ and others can't run directly in the browser and require a separate server. Since the project currently runs without a server, these languages are not supported yet.
docs.limit7: Multi-line input (e.g. stdin-style input like on competitive programming sites) is not supported. Values can only be passed as function parameters.
docs.limit1: Async code (setTimeout, Promise, async/await) isn't supported yet.
docs.limit2: JavaScript class syntax doesn't work yet.
docs.limit3: Max 1,000 recursive calls and 10,000 execution steps.
docs.limit4: Python only supports the standard library. No numpy, pandas, etc.
docs.limit5: Very large inputs may cause execution to stop. Keep test sizes reasonable.
docs.tip6Desc: Feature requests and bug reports are always welcome. Leave them on <link>GitHub Issues</link>. You can also contribute directly via PR.
docs.tip7Desc: Your <link>sponsorship</link> helps support more languages like Java and C++.
docs.changelogDesc: Features are improved based on GitHub Issues. <link>View all issues →</link>
docs.changelog1: Mouse drag panning on the recursion call tree
docs.changelog6: Visualize function calls as stack frames

metadata.title: Recursive — Track Your Algorithms
metadata.description: Watch your code run step by step — line highlighting, variable tracking, and call tree visualization.
